title: Cat Walking with a Drunken Gait: Causes and Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat stumbling or wobbly? Learn why a drunken gait is a high-priority emergency and what steps you need to take for your pet's safety.
content_html:
TL;DR: A sudden drunken gait in cats, known as ataxia, is a high-priority emergency requiring immediate veterinary care to rule out life-threatening toxins, trauma, or neurological issues.

What does it mean if my cat is walking with a drunken gait?

When a cat walks as if they are intoxicated—stumbling, swaying, or falling over—it is medically referred to as ataxia. Ataxia is not a disease itself, but rather a clinical sign that something is interfering with your cat's nervous system. It can affect the legs, the head, or the entire body, leading to a profound loss of coordination and balance.

Is my cat's loss of balance and coordination a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. A sudden onset of a drunken gait is a veterinary emergency.
  • This symptom can be caused by life-threatening issues such as toxin ingestion (like antifreeze or certain plants), head trauma, strokes, or severe neurological infections.
  • Your cat needs to be evaluated by a professional immediately; do not wait for symptoms to "clear up" on their own, as many causes are time-sensitive.

How can taking a video of my cat help the veterinary team?

  • If you can safely do so without delaying your trip to the clinic, capture a brief video of your cat attempting to walk.
  • A video allows the doctor to see the exact nature of the lack of coordination, such as a head tilt, high-stepping, or hind-end weakness.
  • This visual evidence helps the vet quickly localize the problem in the brain, inner ear, or spinal cord during the triage process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cerebellar lesions can produce ataxia and dysmetria, resulting in an uncoordinated, drunken gait. Vestibular dysfunction may cause ipsilateral falling, rolling, or circling. Metabolic disorders, such as diabetic neuropathy, can manifest as weakness, ataxia, and muscle atrophy; in cats, this may present as a plantigrade stance due to tibial nerve dysfunction. If abnormalities are not found during head evaluation, a lesion is likely located in the spinal cord, peripheral nerves, or muscles.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1217)

faqs.question: What are common causes of sudden wobbliness in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include vestibular disease, exposure to toxins (human meds, lilies, or chemicals), ear infections, spinal injuries, or metabolic issues like low blood sugar.

faqs.question: Can an indoor cat still be at risk for toxins?
faqs.answer: Yes. Indoor cats can encounter household hazards such as essential oil diffusers, cleaning products, or toxic houseplants like lilies.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat in a secure, well-padded carrier to prevent them from falling or injuring themselves further during the drive. Keep the environment quiet and dark to reduce stress.
